19. Review whether the organization is preserving liquidity by delaying legitimate obligations.
20. State whether runway is enough to protect service delivery without destabilizing the organization.
21. Determine whether the next major payment cycle is covered without relying on new money.
22. Review whether runway is sufficient to handle a funding gap longer than one cycle.
23. Assess whether the organization needs immediate liquidity discipline or structural funding redesign.
24. Determine whether the current cash position is a genuine cushion or a temporary snapshot.
25. State whether runway is strong, adequate, short, or critical.
26. Determine whether the runway is measured correctly after excluding non-usable balances.
27. Review whether management can materially extend runway through controllable actions.
28. Assess whether the cash picture changes materially under a conservative scenario.
29. Identify whether reserve coverage and runway are aligned or sending different messages.
30. Conclude whether the liquidity base supports sustainability or exposes the organization to near-term risk.
Income Stability and Funding Quality Focus
1. Review the reliability, recurrence, and predictability of funding streams.
2. Separate renewable income from one-time grants and ad hoc support.
3. Assess donor concentration and dependency on a small number of funders.
4. Review whether recurring income is sufficient to support recurring cost base.
5. Determine whether grant renewals are highly probable, possible, or uncertain.
6. Identify whether restricted income is creating a mismatch between cash inflows and operating needs.
7. Review whether funding is lumpy, seasonal, or volatile.
8. Assess whether management has clear evidence of future income pipeline strength.
9. Determine whether the organization depends on good news, goodwill, or repeated fundraising campaigns to survive.
10. Review whether the funding model is diversified enough to absorb a shock.
11. Identify whether concentration risk sits with donors, contracts, geography, or funding type.
12. Review whether recoverable grants or reimbursements are subject to approval risk.
13. Determine whether income quality supports sustainability or only temporary coverage.
14. Compare recurring income to recurring cost commitments.
15. State whether the income model is durable, fragile, or highly exposed.
16. Assess whether funding renewal depends on factors outside management’s control.
17. Identify whether the organization has enough recurring income to preserve reserves rather than consume them.
18. Review whether donor concentration risk is worsened by a short runway or fixed-cost burden.
19. Determine whether funding timing forces the organization into repeated short-term survival planning.
20. State whether the income base is broad, narrow, stable, or vulnerable.
21. Review whether the organization relies too much on a small number of annual decisions.
22. Assess whether income diversification is improving, flat, or deteriorating.
23. Determine whether the funding model can withstand the loss of a major source.
24. Review whether management has a credible plan to replace expiring funding.
25. State whether the income base supports reserve replenishment or drains reserves over time.
26. Determine whether multi-year commitments or renewal trends make the outlook more predictable.
27. Review whether support from donors is episodic or repeatable.
28. Assess whether the organization has enough funding flexibility to match its cost base.
29. Determine whether the current mix of funding is strong enough to justify confidence.
30. Conclude whether the funding structure is sustainable, transitional, or high-risk.
Spending Flexibility Review Focus
1. Review how much of the cost base is fixed versus variable.
2. Determine how quickly spending can be reduced if income weakens.
3. Assess whether payroll, facilities, program commitments, and contractual obligations constrain flexibility.
4. Review whether management has discretion to slow projects, pause hires, or defer nonessential expenditure.
5. Identify whether cost structure is aligned with recurring income or built for growth assumptions.
6. Assess whether the organization can right-size quickly without damaging mission delivery.
7. Review whether savings actions would be immediate, delayed, or disruptive.
8. Determine whether spending behavior suggests discipline or structural rigidity.
9. Review whether any cost commitments extend beyond current-year funding comfort.
10. Assess whether the organization can absorb inflation without eroding reserves.
11. Review whether overhead, support, and compliance costs are sustainable within the income base.
12. Determine whether the cost structure is resilient enough for a funding shock.
13. Identify whether reserve usage is covering a cost structure that should instead be adjusted.
14. State whether the organization has real cost flexibility or only theoretical flexibility.
15. Conclude whether the spending model helps or harms sustainability.
16. Assess whether the budget structure includes enough elasticity to respond to stress quickly.
17. Review whether staffing commitments are scalable or locked in.
18. Determine whether program delivery can be slowed without breaching donor obligations or mission commitments.
19. Identify whether rising fixed costs are reducing strategic options.
20. State whether cost flexibility is strong, moderate, or constrained.
21. Review whether leadership has realistic levers to preserve reserves when income weakens.
22. Determine whether the current cost base requires stronger funding than is likely to recur.
23. Assess whether short-term savings would materially extend runway.
24. Review whether the organization is carrying enough variable cost structure to absorb shocks.
25. Conclude whether spending behavior is supporting or undermining sustainability.
Restricted and Designated Reserve Review Focus
1. Separate unrestricted reserves from restricted cash and designated reserves with absolute clarity.
2. Review whether management is presenting usable reserves or merely total net assets.
3. Determine whether board designations reduce practical flexibility too aggressively.
4. Assess whether donor restrictions constrain reserve access during a genuine crisis.
5. Review whether reserve reporting includes balances that cannot be spent on core operations.
6. Identify any inconsistency between policy labels and actual useability.
7. Determine whether designated reserves should still be counted as available in a severe stress event.
8. Review whether restricted reserves are being confused with true operating protection.
9. Assess whether reserve structure creates a false sense of coverage.
10. Determine whether the organization can actually deploy reserves quickly and lawfully when needed.
11. Review whether designated balances are appropriate given the current risk profile.
12. Identify whether reserve policy should be revised to reflect cash accessibility, not only net asset category.
13. State the portion of reserves that is truly available for continuity support.
14. Review whether reserve composition supports survival or only accounting presentation.
15. Explain clearly how restrictions alter the real protection provided by the reserve base.
16. Determine whether board-designated reserves are too rigid for a crisis response.
17. Review whether a reserve label masks donor conditions or legal limitations.
18. Assess whether reserve reporting changes materially when restricted and designated balances are removed.
19. Determine whether the organization has enough freely deployable resources for immediate continuity needs.
20. State whether reserve accessibility is high, partial, or weak.
21. Review whether reserve funds are held in accounts that can actually be moved and used in time.
22. Determine whether policy and practice align on reserve use.
23. Assess whether the reserve structure is simple enough for a fast decision in a crisis.
24. Review whether the reserve base is inflated by balances that are only theoretically available.
25. Conclude whether the reserve structure supports practical resilience or merely accounting clarity.
Net Asset and Accumulated Result Review Focus
1. Review unrestricted, restricted, and designated net assets where applicable.
2. Determine whether the organization is building capital or consuming it.
3. Assess whether accumulated surpluses are genuine resilience or are offset by hidden obligations.
4. Identify whether deficits are temporary, cyclical, or structural.
5. Review whether net asset growth is supported by recurring performance or one-time events.
6. Determine whether reserve growth is intentional and disciplined.
7. Assess whether board designations weaken practical flexibility.
8. Review whether net asset movement aligns with mission delivery and funding model.
9. Identify whether the balance sheet can support uncertainty, emergencies, or delayed grants.
10. Compare current reserves with prior years and policy targets.
11. Determine whether reserve growth is sufficient to match the risk profile.
12. Check whether deficits are explained transparently.
13. State whether the organization has a strong, moderate, weak, or impaired capital base.
14. Distinguish accounting net assets from practical operating resilience.
15. Explain the significance in a way a board member can use immediately.
16. Review whether net asset categories are being used consistently across periods.
17. Identify whether reserve policy is aligned with actual risk exposure.
18. Determine whether future mission capacity is protected or eroding.
19. Assess whether net asset composition is too rigid to support response to shocks.
20. Evaluate whether unrestricted reserves are sufficient to sustain core operations.
21. Determine whether accumulated balance movements are driven by recurring operations or timing artifacts.
22. Review whether a positive net asset position is actually usable in an emergency.
23. Identify whether the organization has enough capital to remain calm through one difficult year.
24. Determine whether net assets are improving fast enough to restore confidence.
25. Conclude whether the capital base supports durability, caution, or recovery planning.
26. Assess whether surpluses are being retained or effectively consumed by future obligations.
27. Determine whether negative net assets, if present, are temporary or symptomatic of structural strain.
28. Review whether accumulated results explain reserve behavior better than current-year movement alone.
29. Identify whether capital strength is improving faster or slower than the risk profile.
30. State whether the capital base is aligned with the organization’s strategic and operational needs.
Trend Review Focus
1. Compare current balances to prior month, prior quarter, and prior year where available.
2. Identify major movements in cash, reserves, receivables, payables, debt, and net assets.
3. Separate recurring change from one-time disruption.
4. Review whether growth in assets is matched by growth in obligations.
5. Review whether liquidity is strengthening or deteriorating over time.
6. Compare current financial position to budget expectations or forecasts if available.
7. Identify breakpoints where behavior changed materially.
8. Review whether donor concentration has increased or diversified.
9. Detect whether liabilities are creeping upward faster than resources.
10. Review whether reserves are drifting below policy or target levels.
11. Compare current ratios to previous periods for structural movement.
12. Identify whether the organization is stable, recovering, or under pressure.
13. Review whether structural changes explain the movement better than operating performance.
14. State the trend in direct language and support it with the data.
15. Avoid overreading one period without contextual support.
16. Identify if balance sheet improvement is supported by cash or only by accounting timing.
17. Look for repeated end-of-period patterns that suggest temporary optics.
18. Describe whether the trend is benign, cautionary, or concerning.
19. Capture whether the direction of travel matches leadership claims.
20. Highlight whether current performance is cyclical or structural.
21. Review whether reserve depletion is a one-off response or a repeated operating habit.
22. Determine whether trends suggest recovery, stagnation, or creeping fragility.
23. Compare current resilience with the prior year’s ability to absorb shocks.
24. Identify whether apparently stable results hide deterioration in reserve coverage.
25. State whether the trajectory supports confidence in future sustainability.
26. Look for repeated year-end patterns that suggest temporary cleanup rather than durable improvement.
27. Assess whether reserve behavior is consistent with management’s narrative.
28. Determine whether the organization is moving toward stronger resilience or toward dependence on external timing.
29. Review whether trends in funding, cost base, and reserves reinforce each other or conflict.
30. Conclude whether the direction of travel is favorable, neutral, or worrying.
Scenario and Stress Review Focus
1. What happens if a major grant is delayed?
2. What happens if one major donor reduces support?
3. What happens if spending rises faster than expected?
4. What happens if receivables are collected later than planned?
5. What happens if liabilities are discovered late?
6. What happens if foreign exchange moves against the organization?
7. What happens if reserve policy is tightened?
8. What happens if payroll costs increase?
9. What happens if program demand spikes unexpectedly?
10. What happens if the organization cannot refinance or extend borrowing?
11. What happens if inflation increases faster than donor budgets can absorb?
12. What happens if a country office loses a key funder with little notice?
13. What happens if restrictions block access to balances during a cash crisis?
14. What happens if management must freeze hiring or cut programs to preserve continuity?
15. What happens if a one-time donor windfall does not repeat next year?
16. What happens if reserve consumption continues for another 12 months?
17. What happens if the organization must carry more working capital to secure service continuity?
18. What happens if partner payments are delayed by upstream funding issues?
19. What happens if fixed commitments remain while revenue weakens?
20. What happens if the board insists on a stronger reserve target while income remains volatile?
21. What happens if a donor changes approval rules or reimbursements slow materially?
22. What happens if the organization loses flexibility because most funds are restricted?
23. What happens if emergency response costs rise just when funding softens?
24. What happens if the organization must operate through a period of political or operational disruption?
25. What happens if management must choose between preserving reserves and preserving program delivery?
The workflow should be able to frame these questions even when only the balance sheet is available, because board quality analysis depends on anticipating stress rather than merely describing current balances.
26. Use scenarios to test whether reserves are genuinely protective.
27. Use scenarios to expose the weakest point in the funding model.
28. Use scenarios to identify how much flexibility management really has.
29. Use scenarios to judge whether policy targets are realistic.
30. Use scenarios to translate reserve analysis into practical governance decisions.
Sustainability Judgment Framework
1. Strong: the organization has adequate reserves, diversified income, manageable fixed costs, and sufficient runway to absorb a meaningful shock.
2. Acceptable: the organization is stable under current conditions but would need monitoring because resilience is not yet robust.
3. Fragile: the organization has some protection, but a moderate funding delay or cost increase could quickly require action.
4. Critical: the organization has insufficient reserves or runway to absorb a plausible shock without service disruption or emergency intervention.
5. Use evidence from reserves, runway, funding concentration, spending rigidity, and liability pressure to place the organization in one of these categories.
6. Avoid treating the category as a label only; explain the specific drivers behind it.
7. Distinguish present condition from future vulnerability if current reserves are being consumed.
8. Explain whether sustainability depends on management execution, donor timing, or structural business-model strength.
9. State whether the current condition is improving or deteriorating.
10. Identify whether one major stressor would move the organization into a different category.
11. Clarify what level of funding uncertainty the organization can tolerate.
12. Clarify whether management needs immediate corrective action or disciplined monitoring.
13. Clarify whether the board should intervene now or set a tighter threshold for future review.
14. Make the judgment understandable without technical accounting language.
15. Tie the judgment to mission continuity, not just balance sheet appearance.
16. State whether the organization is resilient enough to survive ordinary volatility or only a narrow set of favorable conditions.
17. Explain whether resilience comes from reserves, income quality, flexibility, or all three.
18. Identify whether sustainability is likely to improve without structural change or only with deliberate intervention.
19. Distinguish between a stable present and a secure future.
20. State whether the current sustainability position deserves confidence, caution, or escalation.
Reserve Adequacy Framework
1. Measure reserve coverage in months of operating expense.
2. Compare reserve coverage to the organization’s risk profile and funding volatility.
3. Determine whether reserves are enough to bridge a realistic delay in income.
4. Assess whether reserves can cover fixed commitments, not just variable costs.
5. Check whether reserves are accessible or mostly locked in restrictions, designations, or illiquid assets.
6. Compare reserve level to board policy target and explain deviations.
7. Determine whether reserve usage rules are clear for a crisis scenario.
8. Review whether reserve levels are being rebuilt at a pace consistent with risk appetite.
9. Assess whether reserves are being depleted to fund recurring deficits.
10. Determine whether reserves provide actual operating protection or only accounting comfort.
11. Review whether reserve coverage remains adequate after excluding one-off income.
12. Assess whether reserve adequacy changes materially when inflation or expenditure growth is considered.
13. Identify whether reserve policy is too lenient, too strict, or aligned with reality.
14. State the practical consequence of the current reserve position.
15. Conclude whether reserve strength is sufficient, borderline, or inadequate.
16. Determine whether reserve coverage is enough to bridge the gap between recurring income and recurring obligations.
17. Assess whether reserves can absorb the loss of a major funding source without triggering crisis.
18. Review whether policy coverage is based on current spend, normalized spend, or a modified operating base.
19. Determine whether reserve targets reflect present risk conditions or outdated assumptions.
20. State whether reserve adequacy should be maintained, strengthened, or urgently rebuilt.
Control and Disclosure Review Focus
1. Assess whether reserve and sustainability information is supported by reconciliations and schedules.
2. Review whether disclosure notes are sufficient to explain restrictions, contingencies, commitments, and related-party transactions.
3. Check whether reserve classifications are consistent from period to period.
4. Review whether board-designated reserves are disclosed clearly and separately from donor restrictions.
5. Examine whether key assumptions are documented and reasonable.
6. Identify whether the notes are complete enough for an informed reader.
7. Review whether management commentary matches the financial data.
8. Identify whether cut-off, accrual, or reclassification issues show weak control discipline.
9. Review whether supporting evidence is accessible and traceable.
10. Determine whether financial reporting is stable, disciplined, and decision-grade.
11. Identify whether audit committee attention is needed on any account or disclosure.
12. Review whether any recurring problems suggest process weakness rather than isolated error.
13. Check whether statements are transparent enough for donors and regulators.
14. State whether disclosure quality is strong, moderate, or weak.
15. Recommend stronger control where needed without overreacting to minor issues.
16. Review whether policy exceptions are documented and approved.
17. Check whether evidence trails are sufficient to recreate major balances.
18. Determine whether the reporting environment inspires confidence.
19. Review whether management explanations are consistent across different reports.
20. Determine whether disclosure is adequate for external accountability.
21. Confirm that reserve policy is actually being followed, not just mentioned.
22. Confirm that sustainability commentary is anchored to numbers, not generic reassurance.
23. Confirm that board papers clearly separate protected reserves from available reserves.
24. Confirm that any going concern or continuity language is addressed consistently with the financial evidence.
25. Confirm that the reporting package is sufficiently transparent for stakeholder reliance.
26. Identify whether the reserve narrative is consistent with the cash forecast and budget assumptions.
27. Check whether any limitations should be disclosed explicitly rather than hidden in footnotes.
28. Assess whether disclosure quality is strong enough to support donor confidence and board action.
29. Determine whether the finance team can recreate the reserve calculation from the supporting records.
30. State whether disclosure supports clarity, challenge, and accountability.
Governance Review Focus
1. Determine whether the board is receiving the right information at the right level of detail.
2. Check whether management commentary answers the real risks rather than repeating balances.
3. Review whether the audit committee has visibility into liquidity, reserves, and restrictions.
4. Identify whether policy thresholds exist for reserves, liquidity, and borrowing.
5. Check whether management is complying with those policies or explaining exceptions.
6. Review whether governance oversight is proactive or reactive.
7. Determine whether board designations are consistent with strategy and risk appetite.
8. Review whether donor concentration and cash stress are being escalated in time.
9. Check whether control weaknesses are tracked to closure.
10. Identify whether the board has enough insight to ask the right questions.
11. Review whether management reports distinguish actual risk from routine fluctuations.
12. Determine whether governance needs a stronger dashboard or narrative.
13. Assess whether the finance function is enabling oversight or only producing reports.
14. Review whether the board would be surprised by any of the findings.
15. Determine whether leadership can defend the current position to external stakeholders.
16. Identify whether governance structure supports fast response to stress.
17. Review whether the organization’s accountability chain is clear.
18. State whether governance is strong, acceptable, or in need of strengthening.
19. Determine whether the board is getting early warning rather than post-fact explanations.
20. Assess whether oversight culture matches the organization’s size and complexity.
21. Determine whether the board is seeing reserve trends early enough to change decisions before crisis.
22. Review whether management has a habit of explaining away repeated reserve pressure instead of addressing it.
23. Check whether the board is asking about survivability, not just budget compliance.
24. Determine whether the organization has a credible process for escalating sustainability risk.
25. State whether governance is strong enough to support confidence in the reserve strategy.
26. Review whether the board has the right thresholds to trigger action before reserves become too thin.
27. Determine whether governance papers are concise enough to drive decision-making.
28. Assess whether the finance function is helping the board understand practical resilience.
29. Identify whether the board is relying on too much reassurance and too little evidence.
30. State whether governance support for reserve management is mature, partial, or weak.
Risk Flag Categories
1. Reserve inadequacy risk.
2. Liquidity risk.
3. Runway risk.
4. Funding concentration risk.
5. Spending rigidity risk.
6. Restricted access risk.
7. Debt service risk.
8. Cash conversion risk.
9. Liability understatement risk.
10. Control weakness risk.
11. Disclosure weakness risk.
12. Continuity risk.
13. Forecasting risk.
14. Mission delivery risk.
15. Timing mismatch risk.
16. Refinancing risk.
17. Accrual completeness risk.
18. Counterparty concentration risk.
19. Reputational risk.
20. Governance risk.
21. Inflation pressure risk.
22. Overdependence on one-time funding risk.
23. Hidden obligation risk.
24. Structural deficit risk.
25. Capital rigidity risk.
26. Restricted liquidity illusion risk.
27. Emergency response risk.
28. Program scaling risk.
29. Cost base inflexibility risk.
30. Scenario shock risk.
31. Reserve drawdown risk.
32. Renewal risk.
33. Program concentration risk.
34. Foreign exchange exposure risk.
35. Access-timing risk.
36. Policy non-compliance risk.
37. Sustainability overstatement risk.
38. Mission interruption risk.
39. Funding model fragility risk.
40. Board oversight gap risk.
Recommended Questions for Management
1. How many months of operating expense do our reserves cover?
2. Which balances are truly available in an emergency?
3. What would happen if our largest donor delayed payment by one cycle?
4. What would happen if one major grant were not renewed?
5. Are reserves being replenished or used for recurring shortfalls?
6. Which costs could be reduced quickly if income weakened?
7. Are any liabilities or commitments missing from the books?
8. Are any balances restricted, designated, or otherwise unavailable for core operations?
9. Do we have a credible cash runway forecast for the next 12 months?
10. Which assumptions in the sustainability model are most uncertain?
11. Are we relying on one-time income that will not recur?
12. Are receivables and reimbursements delaying real cash access?
13. Are there unusual journal entries or reserve transfers that need explanation?
14. Are there any covenant, donor, or legal conditions affecting reserve access?
15. What actions are being taken to rebuild or protect reserves?
16. What spending can be paused without harming mission delivery?
17. Which program commitments are hardest to unwind if funding weakens?
18. What scenario would force an emergency response first?
19. Which balances should the board focus on this quarter?
20. Are there any emerging funding risks we have not yet quantified?
21. What is the back-up plan if the forecast proves optimistic?
22. Are reserve targets still appropriate for our current risk profile?
23. Are board-designated funds helping or limiting resilience?
24. What would management do if cash fell below the next threshold?
25. What evidence should be added to support a stronger sustainability conclusion?
26. Which costs are already committed regardless of funding changes?
27. Which revenue sources are most vulnerable to delay, refusal, or non-renewal?
28. What actions would materially extend runway in the next 90 days?
29. Which balances should be treated as unavailable when building the reserve calculation?
30. Which assumptions would change the reserve conclusion from acceptable to fragile?
Recommended Questions for the Board
1. Are reserves large enough for our actual risk profile?
2. Do we have enough unrestricted liquidity to sustain operations safely?
3. Is funding too concentrated in a few donors or grants?
4. Are reserve definitions clear and consistently applied?
5. Is management presenting sustainability transparently?
6. Do we need a stronger reserve policy or liquidity policy?
7. Are we relying too much on timing gaps or delayed settlements?
8. Is the organization financially resilient enough to sustain mission delivery?
9. Should we request deeper analysis on any particular account or fund?
10. Are there warning signs that require board action now?
11. Is the current capital base adequate for strategic plans?
12. Are there areas where policy or oversight needs updating?
13. Is the finance team producing enough clarity for governance?
14. Are concentration risks increasing faster than expected?
15. Are control weaknesses recurring?
16. Is the organization making trade-offs that weaken future stability?
17. Do we need an external review of a specific risk area?
18. Is the board receiving enough early warning before stress becomes crisis?
19. Are we asking the right questions about unrestricted resources?
20. Do we have confidence in the assumptions behind the reported position?
21. Should reserve thresholds be revised?
22. Should we require deeper disclosure on restrictions or debt?
23. Are we comfortable with the current level of dependence on timing or donor advances?
24. Is the organization’s risk appetite still appropriate?
25. What action should the board expect management to take next?
26. Are we comfortable that the board would see a problem early enough to respond?
27. Do we know which trigger would force an emergency discussion?
28. Should reserve monitoring be more frequent or more formal?
29. Are the current resilience assumptions still valid after recent funding changes?
30. What additional oversight would improve confidence in the reserve strategy?
Decision-Making Output
1. The assistant should not stop at analysis.
2. It should convert the balance sheet into decisions and oversight priorities.
3. For each major issue, the output should identify what it means, why it matters, and who should act.
4. If the issue is operational, suggest management follow-up.
5. If the issue is financial policy related, suggest finance leadership or the audit committee.
6. If the issue is strategic, suggest board review.
7. If the issue is compliance related, suggest donor, grant, legal, or audit escalation where appropriate.
8. The output should answer the practical questions of whether the NGO needs to conserve cash, chase collections, renegotiate terms, rebuild reserves, reduce commitments, or strengthen controls.
9. It should make decision paths obvious rather than hidden inside technical commentary.
10. It should also note where no action is needed so leadership can focus only on what matters.
11. Every recommendation should be concrete enough to assign to a person or committee.
12. Every recommendation should have a reason tied to risk or materiality.
13. Recommendations should distinguish between immediate actions, short-term monitoring, and strategic fixes.
14. Recommendations should be sequenced by urgency and likely impact on continuity.
15. The action plan should highlight what will reduce risk fastest.
16. Where reserves are low, the response should focus on runway extension and funding protection.
17. Where sustainability is fragile, the response should focus on funding diversification and cost flexibility.
18. Where liabilities or commitments threaten the reserve base, the response should focus on obligation review and cash protection.
19. Where reserve policy is weak, the response should focus on governance and policy refresh.
20. Where the situation is stable, the response should focus on monitoring and early warning thresholds.
21. Management actions should be operationally specific, not vague encouragement.
22. Board actions should be framed as oversight decisions, not management tasks.
23. Policy actions should define what threshold or rule needs to change.
24. If no action is needed, the assistant should say why monitoring alone is sufficient.
25. The output should help leadership prioritize effort where risk reduction is greatest.
Narrative Output Standard
1. Begin with the headline conclusion.
2. State the strongest indicator of health or stress.
3. State the biggest constraint on flexibility.
4. State whether reserves are adequate.
5. State whether liabilities appear complete.
6. State whether there are anomalies needing review.
7. State whether the position is strengthening or weakening.
8. Keep the first paragraph board-ready.
9. Keep the second paragraph management-useful.
10. Keep the third paragraph action-oriented.
11. If confidence is limited, say so plainly.
12. If the position is mixed, explain the mix clearly.
13. If no major issue exists, say that the position appears stable but not risk-free.
14. If issues exist, rank them by urgency.
15. Write so the summary can be copied directly into a board pack.
16. Keep the first sentence definitive and the supporting sentences explanatory.
17. Avoid burying the conclusion under technical detail.
18. Make the practical meaning of reserve coverage explicit.
19. Distinguish current strength from future endurance.
20. Make it obvious what leadership should worry about first.
21. Lead with the answer, then explain the evidence.
22. Use wording that does not need reinterpretation by finance staff.
23. Keep the narrative honest about any limitations in the data.
24. Be clear when the position is solid and when it only appears solid.
25. Make the summary suitable for direct inclusion in governance materials.
Writing Style Standard
1. Use precise but plain language.
2. Avoid vague praise and generic filler.
3. Write in complete sentences that can be copied into reports.
4. Keep bullets structured and easy to scan.
5. Use financial terminology only when it improves accuracy.
6. Explain technical terms in context when necessary.
7. Keep the tone calm and professional.
8. Make uncertainty visible.
9. Keep the board version shorter and the analyst version deeper.
10. Make every sentence earn its place.
11. Prefer active voice where possible.
12. Use consistent terms for restrictions, reserves, liquidity, runway, and sustainability.
13. Avoid overcomplicated phrasing that obscures meaning.
14. Make the narrative readable out loud in a meeting.
15. Ensure the same language could be used in a memo, board pack, or audit response.
16. Avoid jargon that only accountants understand unless necessary.
17. Use concrete verbs such as assess, verify, explain, flag, and recommend.
18. Keep phrasing stable so future revisions remain comparable.
19. Do not overstate certainty when evidence is partial.
20. Do not understate risk when the evidence shows real fragility.
21. Keep the language reusable across finance, governance, and donor settings.
22. Use stable labels so reports remain comparable across periods.
23. Prefer clarity over elegance when the two conflict.
24. Make it easy for a reader to find the judgment and the reason behind it.
25. The writing should sound like a disciplined finance leader, not a generic summary generator.
26. Keep sentences direct enough for board packs, memos, and audit responses.
27. Avoid inflated language that makes routine issues sound severe.
28. Avoid soft language that hides a serious sustainability problem.
29. Maintain the same terminology across reserve, liquidity, and continuity sections.
30. Ensure the style supports confidence and action, not just description.
Validation Checklist
1. Exactly one column is used.
2. The title row is present and accurate.
3. Purpose is clear and world-class in scope.
4. Scope reflects real NGO reserve and sustainability analysis.
5. Inputs are complete and practical.
6. Preparation controls reduce false conclusions.
7. Workflow steps are logically sequenced.
8. Reserve coverage, runway, and sustainability are covered.
9. Restricted fund treatment is explicit.
10. Income stability, spending flexibility, and liability pressure are reviewed.
11. Trend and scenario analysis are included.
12. Governance and disclosure quality are included.
13. Board and management questions are practical.
14. Output structure is reusable and decision-grade.
15. Markdown rendering should remain clean in copy block format.
16. No duplicate sections are present.
17. No trailing blank rows appear.
18. All
tags are placed where line separation improves readability.
19. The output can be reused in documents and prompts without reformatting.
20. The workflow remains clear even after expansion.
21. Reserve adequacy is evaluated in months of coverage and in practical access terms.
22. Sustainability is judged in a way that supports board action.
23. The output distinguishes immediate survival from long-term endurance.
24. The final document can be used directly in governance materials.
25. The overall structure feels complete rather than skeletal.
26. The analysis can be repeated across periods with consistent logic.
27. The final answer remains understandable to non-accountants.
28. The recommendations are actionable, not merely descriptive.
29. The conclusion is traceable to evidence and visible assumptions.
30. The workflow supports a credible, repeatable standard of review.
Quality Standard
1. The final workflow should feel like a premium finance operating playbook rather than a simple checklist.
2. It should be deep enough for experienced analysts, simple enough for leadership readers, and disciplined enough for audit scrutiny.
3. It should help the assistant produce consistent analysis across many NGOs without losing context or rigor.
4. It should support repeatable reporting, stronger judgments, and clearer explanations.
5. It should also make it easy to expand into related workflows for budget variance, donor compliance, cash forecasting, reserve policy, grant reconciliations, or board reporting.
6. It should create confidence that the assistant understands both the accounting and the mission context.
7. It should feel usable immediately in a real finance function, not theoretical or generic.
8. It should provide a standard that other workflows can inherit.
9. It should be detailed enough to drive quality output but not so noisy that it becomes hard to use.
10. It should be durable across organizations, periods, and reporting styles.
11. It should work as a baseline for a broader NGO finance intelligence system.
12. It should be suitable for human review, automation, and hybrid human-AI analysis workflows.
13. It should support board oversight, management action, donor confidence, and audit readiness at the same time.
14. It should preserve analytical rigor while remaining readable and useful.
15. It should make the organization’s true endurance clearer, not simply more polished.
16. It should reduce false reassurance by testing usable reserves against actual risk.
17. It should make sustainability judgment traceable and repeatable.
18. It should help the finance team speak clearly about survival, resilience, and mission continuity.
19. It should remain stable across time so changes in the output reflect real changes in the organization.
20. It should be strong enough to act as a foundation for a broader NGO financial intelligence system.
21. It should avoid unnecessary complexity while preserving depth where it matters.
22. It should be robust enough for serious governance use and approachable enough for operational teams.
23. It should function as a standard reference point for repeat analyses.
24. It should support improvements in both control quality and strategic decision-making.
25. It should feel durable rather than temporary.
Workflow Logic Hierarchy
1. Input context first.
2. Statement integrity second.
3. Liquidity and runway third.
4. Reserve definition and access fourth.
5. Income stability fifth.
6. Spending flexibility sixth.
7. Restricted and designated balances seventh.
8. Debt and financing eighth.
9. Net assets and reserves ninth.
10. Asset quality tenth.
11. Liability completeness eleventh.
12. Trend and scenario review twelfth.
13. Sustainability assessment thirteenth.
14. Governance and disclosure review fourteenth.
15. Recommendations and action priorities fifteenth.
This order matters because reserve and sustainability analysis must start with accessibility and survival, then move into structural endurance, then end with oversight and action.
16. The order reflects how a careful finance leader should reason from immediate protection to long-term resilience.
17. It prevents the analysis from mistaking total reserves for usable protection.
18. It ensures the funding model is considered before concluding sustainability.
19. It ensures spending flexibility is tested before concluding reserve adequacy.
20. It keeps the final judgment grounded in mission continuity, not just accounting labels.
21. It mirrors how a board would actually think about risk under pressure.
22. It ensures the analysis moves from facts to judgment in a disciplined sequence.
23. It keeps short-term survival from being confused with long-term health.
24. It puts policy and governance after the operational test, not before it.
25. It allows the final recommendation to be strong, specific, and defensible.
Management Action Framework
1. If reserves are weak, prioritize reserve rebuilding and expenditure discipline.
2. If runway is short, prioritize cash preservation and funding timing control.
3. If income is concentrated, prioritize diversification and renewal planning.
4. If spending is rigid, prioritize cost review and flexibility planning.
5. If restricted balances are high, prioritize fund mapping and access clarity.
6. If debt is elevated, prioritize covenant review and repayment planning.
7. If liabilities are incomplete, prioritize cut-off and accrual testing.
8. If anomalies exist, prioritize investigation and evidence review.
9. If sustainability is fragile, prioritize scenario planning and contingency design.
10. If governance is weak, prioritize reporting discipline and oversight clarity.
11. If no major issue exists, prioritize monitoring and early warning thresholds.
12. Every action should include owner, timing, and expected outcome.
13. Every action should reduce specific risk, not create additional complexity.
14. The goal is practical control improvement, not cosmetic reporting.
15. The reserve plan should be tied to actual risk, not a symbolic target.
16. The funding plan should address concentration and renewal risk.
17. The cost plan should increase flexibility rather than merely cut costs.
18. The board plan should specify clear thresholds for escalation.
19. The finance plan should improve confidence in continuity, not just presentation.
20. The action plan should be easy to track and review in subsequent reporting cycles.
21. Management should know which lever to pull first in a stress event.
22. Action items should be realistic for the organization’s capacity and timeline.
23. Actions should be arranged by urgency and impact, not by convenience.
24. Each action should be associated with a clear risk it is intended to reduce.
25. The framework should be usable in live management decisions, not only in reports.
Board Pack Translation
1. The same underlying analysis should be convertible into a concise board version.
2. The board version should retain the headline, the three most important findings, the three most important risks, and the three most important actions.
3. It should avoid account-level noise unless it changes governance conclusions.
4. It should translate financial data into oversight language, such as runway, flexibility, restrictions, concentration, reserve adequacy, and continuity risk.
5. It should tell the board what is happening, why it matters, whether the trend is improving or worsening, and what the board should ask management to do.
6. It should avoid technical detail unless a committee specifically needs it.
7. It should be suitable for a meeting pack, a committee memo, or an executive dashboard narrative.
8. It should identify whether the organization is comfortably protected, conditionally protected, or exposed.
9. It should make clear whether reserves are enough to absorb a realistic stress event.
10. It should state whether management has a credible sustainability plan or only a hope of continued support.
11. It should separate urgent matters from items that only need monitoring.
12. It should help directors know which question to ask next.
13. It should be brief enough for a board pack but strong enough to support decisions.
14. It should state whether the current reserve position is a source of confidence or concern.
15. It should be ready for direct insertion into governance papers.
16. It should help the board understand whether action is needed now or later.
17. It should spotlight the most important threshold crossings, if any.
18. It should use plain language that directors can act on immediately.
19. It should support both oversight and accountability without overexplaining technical detail.
20. It should make the board’s role in sustainability clear.
Audit Pack Translation
1. The same workflow should also support audit preparation.
2. It should identify accounts needing support, balances needing reconciliation, disclosures needing expansion, and items needing explanation.
3. It should isolate anomalies, confirm classification logic, and help produce a traceable rationale for key balances.
4. It should surface contingencies, related-party items, and policy judgments that auditors may question.
5. It should help the finance team anticipate requests rather than react to them.
6. It should reduce surprises by forcing clarity before fieldwork or review begins.
7. It should identify whether reserve labels are internally consistent and supported by policy.
8. It should reveal where management commentary may need stronger evidence.
9. It should help explain why certain balances are usable, restricted, or designated.
10. It should flag sustainability matters that may intersect with going concern or continuity discussions.
11. It should support clean schedules and accurate narratives.
12. It should reduce the chance that auditors discover reserve logic problems late in the process.
13. It should help the team prepare answers to questions about access, timing, commitment, and concentration.
14. It should make the underlying assumptions visible and challengeable.
15. It should be strong enough to improve the quality of the audit conversation.
16. It should help identify where reserve and liquidity explanations may need stronger documentation.
17. It should support the preparation of clear lead schedules and support files.
18. It should help the finance team anticipate scrutiny on estimates, restrictions, and funding dependency.
19. It should reduce the chance of avoidable back-and-forth with auditors.
20. It should improve confidence that reserve and sustainability conclusions are supportable.
Donor Communication Translation
1. The workflow should also support donor-safe communication.
2. That means it should be able to explain financial position in a way that is accurate, transparent, and reassuring without overpromising.
3. It should distinguish between restricted and unrestricted resources and explain why that distinction matters.
4. It should clarify how donor timing, reimbursement lag, or conditional grants affect liquidity without turning every issue into a crisis narrative.
5. It should help leadership communicate responsibly when donors ask whether the organization is stable, responsible, and capable of delivering results.
6. It should avoid language that could falsely imply more flexibility than the organization actually has.
7. It should explain reserve strength without pretending the organization is invulnerable.
8. It should support honest communication about funding concentration and uncertainty.
9. It should help leadership explain the steps being taken to protect mission continuity.
10. It should be able to reassure donors when the position is stable and caution them when the position is fragile.
11. It should distinguish between temporary pressure and deeper structural weakness.
12. It should allow leadership to answer questions without overcomplicating the story.
13. It should preserve credibility by matching external language to the financial evidence.
14. It should help the organization avoid inconsistent messages across donors, board, and auditors.
15. It should be suitable for formal donor correspondence and informal stewardship conversations alike.
16. It should help explain why reserve stewardship matters to mission continuity.
17. It should help distinguish caution from alarm when speaking to funders.
18. It should support transparent discussions about risk without undermining trust.
19. It should keep the organization from overstating available flexibility.
20. It should help leadership communicate with credibility, precision, and restraint.
Scenario Thinking
1. What happens if a major grant is delayed?
2. What happens if one major donor reduces support?
3. What happens if spending rises faster than expected?
4. What happens if receivables are collected later than planned?
5. What happens if liabilities are discovered late?
6. What happens if foreign exchange moves against the organization?
7. What happens if reserve policy is tightened?
8. What happens if payroll costs increase?
9. What happens if program demand spikes unexpectedly?
10. What happens if the organization cannot refinance or extend borrowing?
11. What happens if inflation increases faster than donor budgets can absorb?
12. What happens if a country office loses a key funder with little notice?
13. What happens if restrictions block access to balances during a cash crisis?
14. What happens if management must freeze hiring or cut programs to preserve continuity?
15. What happens if a one-time donor windfall does not repeat next year?
16. What happens if reserve consumption continues for another 12 months?
17. What happens if the organization must carry more working capital to secure service continuity?
18. What happens if partner payments are delayed by upstream funding issues?
19. What happens if fixed commitments remain while revenue weakens?
20. What happens if the board insists on a stronger reserve target while income remains volatile?
21. What happens if a large portion of reserves is tied up in non-cash assets?
22. What happens if management is forced to shift from growth to survival mode?
23. What happens if a delayed reimbursement coincides with a payroll peak?
24. What happens if the next major renewal decision does not materialize as expected?
25. What happens if one or two adverse events occur at the same time rather than separately?
The workflow should be able to frame these questions even when only the balance sheet is available, because board quality analysis depends on anticipating stress rather than merely describing current balances.
26. Use scenarios to test whether reserves are genuinely protective.
27. Use scenarios to expose the weakest point in the funding model.
28. Use scenarios to identify how much flexibility management really has.
29. Use scenarios to judge whether policy targets are realistic.
30. Use scenarios to translate reserve analysis into practical governance decisions.
Confidence and Limitation Handling
1. State when evidence is complete and when it is partial.
2. State when conclusions are strong and when they are provisional.
3. Identify which balances are supported by schedule detail and which are not.
4. Identify when cash is confirmed versus estimated.
5. Identify when receivables are aged versus simply listed.
6. Identify when liabilities are accrued versus assumed.
7. Identify when restrictions are documented versus inferred.
8. Identify when an anomaly is confirmed versus suspected.
9. Identify when trend analysis is robust versus thin.
10. Never bury uncertainty inside polished language.
11. If the data is insufficient, say exactly what is missing.
12. If a conclusion depends on judgment, label it as judgment.
13. If an issue needs more evidence, say so clearly.
14. The assistant should remain helpful even when data quality is imperfect.
15. The answer should help users move forward, not stall on imperfection.
16. Distinguish between missing evidence and actual weakness.
17. Distinguish between a low-confidence conclusion and a negative conclusion.
18. Identify which missing schedule would improve confidence fastest.
19. Note whether the limitation changes the judgment or only reduces certainty.
20. If the reserve calculation depends on an assumption, state the assumption explicitly.
21. If the sustainability assessment depends on forecasted income, state the forecast caveat clearly.
22. If the organization is stable but the evidence is thin, say so plainly.
23. If the organization is fragile and the evidence is strong, say that plainly too.
24. Do not hide uncertainty behind polished language.
25. Leave the reader with a clear understanding of what is known, what is unknown, and what matters most next.
26. If assumptions are used, list the ones that materially affect the conclusion.
27. If management has not provided enough support, make the limitation visible rather than smoothing it over.
28. If the confidence level changes the decision, say so directly.
29. If the evidence supports only a provisional view, mark it clearly.
30. Keep the user informed about whether the analysis is firm, conditional, or limited.
Future Extensions
1. Add a reserve adequacy model with policy thresholds and scenario bands.
2. Add a runway dashboard with months-of-cover calculations and warning levels.
3. Add a restricted versus available reserve reconciliation workflow.
4. Add a funding concentration and renewal risk workflow.
5. Add a fixed-cost rigidity assessment workflow.
6. Add a board report executive-summary generator.
7. Add an anomaly detection workflow for period-end close.
8. Add a donor concentration risk dashboard workflow.
9. Add a continuity and sustainability forecast workflow.
10. Add an audit committee risk memo version.
11. Add a management action tracker linked to each red flag.
12. Add a reserve policy drafting workflow.
13. Add a disclosure completeness review pack.
14. Add a country-office consolidation workflow.
15. Add a narrative generator that translates reserve status into plain-English mission impact.
16. Add a funding shock scenario stress test.
17. Add a cash rescue action plan template.
18. Add a multi-year reserve calibration model.
19. Add a board dashboard with risk indicators and thresholds.
20. Add a donor-safe communication draft for sustainability concerns.
21. Add a control-maturity scoring rubric.
22. Add a close-certification checklist for finance teams.
23. Add a governance escalation matrix.
24. Add a monthly risk heatmap for leadership meetings.
25. Add a reusable prompt pack for different NGO sizes and complexity levels.
26. Add a reserve consumption tracker that flags when the organization is drawing down too fast.
27. Add a funding replacement model that estimates how quickly lost income can be backfilled.
28. Add a spending flexibility model that quantifies which costs can be reduced within 30, 60, and 90 days.
29. Add a reserve access map that distinguishes between legally usable, board-designated, and practically available balances.
30. Add a sustainability scorecard that combines reserves, runway, income concentration, and cost rigidity.
31. Add a liquidity-restriction bridge that shows how accessible cash converts into real operating protection.
32. Add a governance trigger matrix that connects reserve levels to board escalation actions.
33. Add a stress-response playbook that assigns actions for early, moderate, and severe sustainability pressure.
34. Add a donor communication generator for reserve policy and continuity risk.
35. Add a mission continuity dashboard that links financial risk to beneficiary impact.
Final Delivery Standard
1. The finished workflow should be ready for direct reuse in prompt engineering, SOP documentation, analyst playbooks, or internal training materials.
2. It should remain structurally clean when pasted into Markdown tools, chat systems, documentation editors, or knowledge bases.
3. It should preserve the one-column format while carrying enough substance to be genuinely useful in professional work.
4. It should be consistent with the earlier version but significantly richer in depth, governance relevance, and analytical discipline.
5. It should feel like a durable operating standard for NGO reserve and sustainability analysis rather than a one-off response.
6. It should be complete enough that a finance team could adopt it as a working template and refine it over time.
7. It should be robust enough to support both human analysts and AI-assisted review workflows.
8. It should be directly adaptable into a board deck, a controller checklist, or an audit-prep memo with minimal rework.
9. It should work as a professional baseline that can be extended into more specialized workflows without losing consistency.
10. It should allow future users to add sector-specific nuance while keeping the core analytical logic intact.
11. It should be useful whether the user is reviewing one NGO or building a repeatable system across a portfolio.
12. It should remain readable after expansion, with clear structure and stable terminology.
13. It should be substantial enough to stand on its own as a finance operating standard.
14. It should be clear enough that another analyst could follow it without additional instruction.
15. It should be strong enough to support high-stakes governance and accountability use cases.
16. It should make reserve adequacy and sustainability understandable in practical mission terms.
17. It should help leadership see whether the organization is resilient, vulnerable, or at risk of interruption.
18. It should ensure the final narrative is suitable for board, management, donor, and audit contexts without rework.
19. It should be durable across periods and organizations, not dependent on one reporting style.
20. It should feel like a true operating standard rather than a summary template.
21. It should support direct copying into documents without formatting cleanup.
22. It should remain coherent even after further expansions and refinements.
23. It should be detailed enough to guide serious work and simple enough to be reused consistently.
24. It should help standardize reserve and sustainability thinking across the organization.
25. It should be suitable for both recurring reporting and high-stakes one-off reviews.
26. It should preserve the one-column format fully.
27. It should remain board-safe, donor-safe, and audit-aware.
28. It should be ready for immediate practical use.
29. It should be strong enough to support a broader NGO financial intelligence framework.
30. It should feel durable, disciplined, and professional from first use.
Rows: 41
Table 12
NGO Ratio Analysis Workflow — World-Class Assistance
Purpose
1. Design a world-class ratio analysis workflow for NGO financial statements that goes beyond calculation and delivers decision-grade interpretation for executives, boards, audit committees, donors, and finance leaders.
2. Convert raw balance sheet, income statement, cash flow, and fund data into a disciplined view of liquidity, solvency, sustainability, efficiency, flexibility, dependency, and mission capacity.
3. Make the assistant behave like a seasoned nonprofit controller, financial analyst, treasury lead, and board reporting advisor working in one integrated system.
4. Ensure the output does not merely list ratios, but explains what each ratio means, why it matters, what can distort it, and what leadership should do next.
5. Use ratios as a diagnostic system that reveals pressure, resilience, and fragility, not as isolated metrics with no context.
6. Preserve the distinction between accounting presentation and real financial health so the analysis remains trustworthy under scrutiny.
7. Make the output strong enough for board packs, audit prep, donor conversations, management reviews, and internal control monitoring.
8. Ensure the workflow can identify when a ratio is genuinely strong, when it is temporarily inflated, and when it is misleading because of restrictions, accruals, timing, or classification.
9. Make the analysis useful to both specialists and non-specialists by turning technical ratio logic into plain-language insight.
10. Keep the standard clear, rigorous, practical, and directly usable in real NGO governance and finance settings.
11. Treat ratio analysis as an early warning system, not a retrospective scoring exercise.
12. Build a workflow that helps leadership understand current position, trend direction, and future risk in one coherent narrative.
13. Make sure the analysis works when the organization has donor restrictions, grant conditions, seasonal receipts, delayed reimbursements, or multiple funding streams.
14. Ensure the workflow can be reused across reporting periods without changing the core logic.
15. Make the result useful enough that a finance team can adopt it as a standard operating model.
Scope
1. Applies to NGOs, charities, foundations, humanitarian agencies, relief organizations, faith-based institutions, development groups, membership bodies, and other mission-driven not-for-profit entities.
2. Suitable for monthly close packs, quarterly board reports, annual audits, donor reporting, treasury monitoring, reserve reviews, budgeting, forecasting, and internal control oversight.
3. Works for standalone entities, consolidated groups, country offices, branches, project funds, restricted funds, and federated structures with inter-fund balances.
4. Applicable whether reporting follows IFRS, US GAAP not-for-profit guidance, local nonprofit standards, statutory reporting frameworks, or internal management conventions.
5. Must remain useful in both simple and complex accounting environments, from small NGOs with basic statements to large multi-country organizations with layered funding and sophisticated restrictions.
6. Should support organizations with grants, donations, membership fees, service income, government contracts, endowment-like funds, and mixed funding streams.
7. Should work even when the chart of accounts is imperfect, the classification structure is inconsistent, or disclosures are partial.
8. Should remain relevant across programmatic NGOs, advocacy groups, service delivery organizations, emergency response bodies, and large federated nonprofits.
9. Should still provide value when only partial data is available, while becoming richer as more schedules and notes are added.
10. Should be adaptable to country-specific regulatory demands, donor expectations, and internal governance frameworks.
11. Should support organizations with seasonal income, project-based spending, restricted grant cycles, or unpredictable donor timing.
12. Should handle entities that depend on a small number of funders as well as those with diversified income.
13. Should work for organizations with high administrative intensity and for those with lean central functions.
14. Should remain useful when liquidity is constrained by ring-fenced accounts, donor advances, or compliance requirements.
15. Should be sensitive to the fact that nonprofit balance sheets often contain funding structures that are not comparable to commercial businesses.
16. Should support both operational ratio analysis and governance-level ratio interpretation.
17. Should support organizations under stable conditions, rapid growth, restructuring, emergency response, or funding shock.
18. Should be usable for both routine monthly dashboarding and deep-dive board review.
19. Should support one-entity review as well as multi-entity or multi-country ratio comparison.
20. Should be robust enough for professional use without needing a rewrite for each organization.
Assistant Standard
1. Act as a senior nonprofit finance professional with strong judgment, deep attention to detail, and the ability to translate accounting into plain-language insight.
2. Read ratios critically, ask informed follow-up questions, identify missing context, and distinguish calculation from meaning.
3. Understand that a good ratio can hide a weak operating model, and a poor ratio can still be manageable if timing, restrictions, or funding design explain it.
4. Explain what matters to management, what matters to the board, and what matters to auditors without confusing those audiences.
5. Keep the tone exact, calm, professional, and decisive without sounding alarmist or vague.
6. Recognize when a ratio is driven by seasonality, donor timing, or a one-off event rather than by structural performance.
7. Avoid generic corporate commentary that ignores donor restrictions, grant conditions, or mission-specific operating realities.
8. Prefer evidence-based skepticism over assumption, especially where the ratio is affected by judgment, classification, or cut-off.
9. Treat low liquidity, weak coverage, or poor diversification as risks only after considering the entity’s funding model and commitments.
10. Produce conclusions that a finance director, audit committee chair, or executive director could immediately use in a meeting.
11. Understand that some ratios are only meaningful when paired with funding quality, liquidity access, and obligation timing.
12. Remain alert to distortions caused by year-end close behavior, manual journals, or delayed accruals.
13. Separate truly useful signals from ratios that only look impressive because the denominator is temporarily unusual.
14. Be equally willing to identify positive resilience and negative fragility.
15. Make the analysis usable as a governance tool, not merely a technical computation.
16. Translate ratio results into practical questions, not just numerical commentary.
17. Avoid overclaiming certainty where the evidence is incomplete.
18. Explain the difference between apparent strength and operational strength.
19. Treat ratio interpretation as a judgment exercise grounded in evidence.
20. Make sure the final response can stand in a board paper, audit memo, or management pack.
Core Operating Principles
1. Prioritize financial reality over presentation.
2. Separate unrestricted resources from restricted and designated resources.
3. Treat cash as useful only to the extent that it is accessible and not ring-fenced.
4. Treat receivables as uncertain until collectability is tested.
5. Treat liabilities as potentially understated unless completeness is validated.
6. Treat positive net assets as meaningful only after restrictions, designations, and hidden obligations are considered.
7. Treat one-time grants, settlements, or recoveries as non-recurring unless evidence supports permanence.
8. Treat unusual balance movements as signals requiring explanation, not assumptions.
9. Distinguish fact from inference and inference from recommendation.
10. Focus on materiality, mission impact, and continuity rather than accounting noise.
11. Use plain English without losing precision.
12. Make uncertainty visible instead of hiding it in polished prose.
13. Keep conclusions traceable to statements, notes, schedules, and management explanations.
14. Maintain consistency across periods so trends can be trusted.
15. Always ask whether the organization can keep serving its beneficiaries safely.
16. Use ratios as indicators, not as final answers.
17. Compare ratios across time, against budget, and against the organization’s own policy thresholds before judging them.
18. Avoid comparing NGOs to commercial benchmarks unless the structure and funding model are genuinely similar.
19. Treat restricted and designated resources carefully so ratio conclusions do not overstate flexibility.
20. Make sure the interpretation always answers “so what?” rather than stopping at calculation.
21. Do not let a strong ratio conceal weak evidence.
22. Do not let a weak ratio drive alarm if the structure and context explain it credibly.
23. Always distinguish between available, usable, and reported resources.
24. Keep the analysis grounded in mission continuity and governance usefulness.
25. Build the interpretation so it can survive board, donor, and audit challenge.
26. Treat ratio trends as more important than one point in time.
27. Keep a strong boundary between accounting mechanics and management meaning.
28. Preserve caution when the underlying data quality is mixed.
29. Avoid simplistic “good ratio / bad ratio” thinking without context.
30. Use every ratio to support a real decision, not just a descriptive label.
Inputs Required
1. Statement of financial position / balance sheet.
2. Comparative prior-period balance sheet.
3. Statement of activities / income and expense report.
4. Statement of cash flows, if available.
5. Notes to the financial statements.
6. Trial balance or general ledger extract.
7. Fund balance, net asset, or restricted fund schedules.
8. Receivables aging report.
9. Payables aging report.
10. Bank reconciliations or cash control summaries.
11. Debt and lease schedules.
12. Fixed asset register.
13. Grant agreements and donor restriction summaries.
14. Budget and forecast data.
15. Board reports, prior management commentary, and audit committee notes.
16. Any known restructuring, emergency response, legal matter, or major funding event.
17. Any donor concentration or funding dependency analysis.
18. Any reserve policy, liquidity policy, treasury policy, or financial governance policy in force.
19. Any cash flow forecast or runway estimate if available.
20. Any prior ratio analysis or management dashboard used internally.
21. Any covenant calculation worksheet or borrowing compliance pack.
22. Any cost allocation methodology note, because efficiency ratios depend on allocation logic.
23. Any donor reporting schedule that explains restricted and unrestricted income recognition.
24. Any summary of capital commitments, contingent liabilities, or material post-period events.
25. Any historic trend analysis that can help separate structural movement from one-off volatility.
26. Any management narrative that claims improvement, stress, or stability.
27. Any internal benchmark, target, threshold, or policy limit used by leadership.
28. Any note explaining whether funds are held centrally or by country office/project.
29. Any evidence of delayed grant receipts, donor reimbursement lag, or payment delays.
30. Any explanation of unusual accounting policy choices that may affect ratio comparability.
31. Any breakdown of operating costs by program, support, and fundraising functions.
32. Any schedule showing restricted versus unrestricted cash if available.
33. Any related-party balance or intercompany schedule if applicable.
34. Any notes about going concern, covenant pressure, or post-period shocks.
35. Any management summary of recent donor renewals or major funding wins/losses.
Intake Context to Capture
1. Legal name of the reporting entity.
2. Reporting period and comparison periods.
3. Currency and unit of presentation.
4. Consolidated or standalone basis.
5. Accounting framework and classification basis.
6. Audit status: audited, reviewed, or management-prepared.
7. Main funding sources and funding model.
8. Presence of donor restrictions, board designations, or endowment-like balances.
9. Known one-time events affecting the period.
10. Known changes in accounting policy or presentation.
11. Known cash stress, funding gaps, or delayed reimbursements.
12. Major asset purchases, disposals, or write-downs.
13. Staffing or program scale changes.
14. Foreign exchange or multi-currency exposure, if relevant.
15. Country offices, branches, or intercompany balances, if relevant.
16. Whether the board has a reserve target or liquidity benchmark.
17. Whether there are donor conditions, covenants, or grant rules affecting balances.
18. Whether management already suspects any weaknesses or anomalies.
19. Whether the organization is in growth, stable, stress, turnaround, merger, or emergency mode.
20. Whether the funding mix is highly seasonal or unusually concentrated.
21. Whether the organization holds significant restricted cash that distorts liquidity ratios.
22. Whether reserves are governed by a formal policy or remain informal.
23. Whether finance leadership already monitors ratio thresholds internally.
24. Whether the entity is exposed to inflation, exchange-rate volatility, or unstable donor payments.
25. Whether there are known balance sheet distortions caused by timing or presentation choices.
26. Whether management expects a major grant renewal, contract award, or donor transition.
27. Whether the organization has recent history of deficits, covenant pressure, or reserve depletion.
28. Whether support costs are intentionally lean or accidentally underfunded.
29. Whether the organization is operating with high trust from donors or under close scrutiny.
30. Whether the entity’s financial model is mature, evolving, or under structural strain.
31. Whether the current period includes emergency spending, inflation spikes, or currency shocks.
32. Whether the organization has meaningful joint ventures, pass-through funds, or partner settlements.
33. Whether there is a history of late accruals or unrecorded liabilities.
34. Whether any country office is materially stronger or weaker than the group average.
35. Whether management wants a board-safe summary, an audit-safe memo, or an executive dashboard.
Preparation Controls
1. Confirm all dates align across statements and schedules.
2. Confirm comparative numbers are on the same basis and scope.
3. Confirm restricted, designated, and unrestricted balances are clearly separated.
4. Confirm donor advances are not misclassified as revenue before conditions are satisfied.
5. Confirm all major liabilities are recorded through period end.
6. Confirm cash and bank balances reconcile to statements and control records.
7. Confirm receivables aging agrees to the ledger and supporting schedules.
8. Confirm fixed assets and depreciation agree to the balance sheet.
9. Confirm inter-fund or intercompany items are eliminated or explained.
10. Confirm material reclassifications are visible and justified.
11. Confirm large manual journal entries are reviewed.
12. Confirm note disclosures are sufficient to interpret restrictions, commitments, and contingencies.
13. Confirm budget comparisons are made on the correct basis.
14. Confirm foreign exchange effects are considered where needed.
15. Confirm missing evidence is flagged as a limitation rather than assumed away.
16. Confirm unusual balances are not treated as normal without support.
17. Confirm prior-period restatements are understood before trend analysis.
18. Confirm the analysis can withstand board, donor, or audit scrutiny.
19. Confirm ratio formulas are built from consistent definitions across periods.
20. Confirm numerator and denominator treatment is identical across comparative periods.
21. Confirm ratios that depend on restricted resources are not misleadingly presented as unrestricted strength.
22. Confirm one-off balance sheet items are not allowed to distort trend conclusions.
23. Confirm revenue timing and expense timing are aligned when calculating operating ratios.
24. Confirm donor advances, deferred revenue, and grant liabilities are treated appropriately.
25. Confirm the final interpretation is stable enough to be defended to governance readers.
26. Confirm no ratio depends on a denominator that changed because of reclassification rather than real activity.
27. Confirm any scaling factor or unit conversion is applied consistently.
28. Confirm any change in accounting policy is disclosed before comparing periods.
29. Confirm efficiency ratios are not distorted by capital spending or emergency costs.
30. Confirm the ratio set selected is actually relevant to the organization’s model.
31. Confirm that any ratios involving averages use the correct averaging method.
32. Confirm seasonal spikes are identified before conclusions are drawn.
33. Confirm any management adjustment is clearly separated from raw reported numbers.
34. Confirm that all major donors, grants, or funding sources are represented in concentration analysis.
35. Confirm that policy thresholds are understood before any ratio is judged good or bad.
Workflow Steps
1. User supplies statements, notes, and supporting schedules.
2. Assistant identifies scope, period, framework, and reporting context.
3. Assistant extracts the relevant ratio base lines from the statements.
4. Assistant defines each ratio clearly before interpreting it.
5. Assistant calculates liquidity, solvency, leverage, efficiency, and sustainability ratios where data permits.
6. Assistant checks whether each ratio is meaningful in the NGO context.
7. Assistant adjusts interpretation for restrictions, designated funds, grants, and donor timing.
8. Assistant compares ratios against prior periods and policy benchmarks.
9. Assistant identifies trends, breakpoints, and anomalies in the ratio profile.
10. Assistant separates structural strength from temporary timing effects.
11. Assistant connects ratios to underlying account movements and funding model.
12. Assistant reviews whether the ratio evidence supports management’s narrative.
13. Assistant assesses risk exposure and mission implications.
14. Assistant writes a clear plain-English interpretation for management and board readers.
15. Assistant identifies red flags, explains their significance, and suggests next questions.
16. Assistant validates logic, completeness, and consistency.
17. Assistant formats the result into a copy-ready one-column workflow structure.
18. Assistant ensures the final narrative is board-safe, donor-safe, and audit-aware.
19. Assistant highlights which ratios are reliable, which are directional, and which are too distorted to rely on alone.
20. Assistant turns the ratio picture into action priorities.
21. Assistant ranks the most important ratios rather than treating all metrics as equally important.
22. Assistant explains whether a ratio problem is urgent, structural, seasonal, or informational.
23. Assistant identifies which department or committee should own follow-up.
24. Assistant preserves comparability so the same framework can be used month after month.
25. Assistant ensures the output is fit for immediate use in governance and management reporting.
26. Assistant determines whether any ratio should be excluded from interpretation because the underlying accounting base is not stable enough.
27. Assistant translates the ratio story into one short headline, three key findings, three key risks, and three key actions.
28. Assistant identifies whether current ratios are trending toward policy thresholds.
29. Assistant specifies whether the issue is solvency, liquidity, structure, efficiency, or sustainability.
30. Assistant closes with a recommendation set that is assigned to the correct owner.
31. Assistant distinguishes between ratio signals and ratio noise.
32. Assistant checks whether the conclusions are robust across multiple periods.
33. Assistant makes uncertainty visible where the ratio base is weak.
34. Assistant identifies whether follow-up needs management action, board attention, or audit support.
